title: Comparison Estimators: ETWFE and BETWFE
text: fetwfe package implements fetwfe() recommended estimator difference--differences staggered adoptions. also exports two related estimators serve comparison baselines: etwfe() — Wooldridge-style extended two-way fixed effects. betwfe() — bridge-penalized ETWFE; like fetwfe() without fusion transformation. vignette demonstrates , simulated data can compare estimator known true treatment effects. background staggered-adoption real-data application recommended fetwfe() estimator, see main vignette. underlying simulation pipeline used , see simulation vignette. methodological details, see Faletto (2025). three estimators (fetwfe(), etwfe(), betwfe()) accept call signature, user familiar fetwfe() can drop etwfe() betwfe() simply changing function name. use *WithSimulatedData() wrappers keep simulation flow concise.

title: Comparison Estimators: ETWFE and BETWFE
text: use genCoefs() + simulateData() pipeline (approach simulation vignette). parameters chosen etwfe() betwfe() well-conditioned: enough cohorts units etwfe() doesn’t run rank-deficiency, enough density true coefficient vector betwfe()’s bridge regularization shrinks toward zero without zeroing everything .
code: sim_coefs <- genCoefs(   G         = 3,   T         = 6,   d         = 2,   density   = 0.5,   eff_size  = 2,   seed      = 20260510 )  sim_data <- simulateData(   sim_coefs,   N            = 120,   sig_eps_sq   = 1,   sig_eps_c_sq = 1,   seed         = 20260510 )  # True treatment effects (we'll compare estimator output to these): true_tes <- getTes(sim_coefs) cat("True overall ATT:", true_tes$att_true, "\n") #> True overall ATT: 3.877778 print(true_tes$actual_cohort_tes) #> [1] 4.800000 3.500000 3.333333

title: Comparison Estimators: ETWFE and BETWFE
text: etwfe() implements Wooldridge-style extended two-way fixed effects estimator: cohort-time dummy interactions estimated OLS, regularization. model’s assumptions produces unbiased point estimates asymptotically exact standard errors. trade-compared fetwfe() variance: fusion penalty, estimator can high-variance -parameterized regimes, errors entirely cohorts small relative number covariates ((d + 1) units per cohort floor). can compare estimated overall ATT truth:
code:
res_etwfe <- etwfeWithSimulatedData(sim_data)  summary(res_etwfe) #> Summary of Extended Two-Way Fixed Effects #> ======================================== #>  #> Overall ATT: 3.8899  (SE = 0.1791, p = 1.498e-104, 95% CI = [3.5388, 4.2410]) #>  #> CATT (preview) [simultaneous 95% CI]: #>  cohort estimate        se   ci_low  ci_high p_value #>       2 4.658986 0.2422635 4.083908 5.234063       0 #>       3 3.773047 0.2080983 3.279070 4.267023       0 #>       4 3.104032 0.2240861 2.572104 3.635960       0 #>  #> Event Study (preview) [simultaneous 95% CI]: #>  event_time n_cohorts estimate        se   ci_low  ci_high p_value #>           0         3 3.368180 0.1994002 2.865330 3.871030       0 #>           1         3 3.855024 0.1926492 3.369199 4.340850       0 #>           2         3 3.447353 0.2684186 2.770452 4.124255       0 #>           3         2 5.046964 0.2749486 4.353595 5.740333       0 #>           4         1 5.864494 0.3432443 4.998895 6.730092       0 #>  #> Model Details: #>   Units (N)           : 120 #>   Time periods (T)    : 6 #>   Treated cohorts (G) : 3 #>   Covariates (d)      : 2 #>   Features (p)        : 62 cat("True ATT:     ", true_tes$att_true, "
") #> True ATT:      3.877778 cat("Estimated ATT:", res_etwfe$att_hat, "
") #> Estimated ATT: 3.88994 cat("Squared error:", (res_etwfe$att_hat - true_tes$att_true)^2, "
") #> Squared error: 0.0001479292

title: Comparison Estimators: ETWFE and BETWFE
text: betwfe() extends etwfe() adding bridge (L_q, 0 < q < 1) regularization penalty cohort-time effects. Compared etwfe(), trades small amount bias lower variance — idea fetwfe(), without fusion transformation fetwfe() applies. betwfe() essentially “fetwfe minus fusion.” Comparing truth etwfe(): bridge penalty betwfe() shrinks estimate toward zero relative etwfe(). regime, produces noticeable bias — textbook bias-variance trade-action. regimes (sparser true effects, noisier data), bias regularization offset reduced variance, betwfe() outperforms etwfe().
code:
res_betwfe <- betwfeWithSimulatedData(sim_data)  summary(res_betwfe) #> Summary of Bridge-Penalized Extended Two-Way Fixed Effects #> ========================================================== #>  #> Overall ATT: 3.7846  (SE = 0.1370, p = 4.325e-168, 95% CI = [3.5162, 4.0530]) #> Selected: TRUE #>  #> CATT (preview) [simultaneous 95% CI]: #>  cohort estimate        se   ci_low  ci_high p_value selected #>       2 4.478828 0.1820991 4.044556 4.913100       0     TRUE #>       3 3.682706 0.1844766 3.242764 4.122648       0     TRUE #>       4 3.071311 0.2010365 2.591877 3.550745       0     TRUE #>  #> Event Study (preview) [simultaneous 95% CI]: #>  event_time n_cohorts estimate        se   ci_low  ci_high p_value #>           0         3 3.261924 0.1874452 2.783997 3.739851       0 #>           1         3 3.712544 0.1722744 3.273298 4.151791       0 #>           2         3 3.339178 0.2297940 2.753274 3.925081       0 #>           3         2 4.926794 0.2158458 4.376454 5.477134       0 #>           4         1 5.763977 0.2314455 5.173862 6.354091       0 #>  #> Model Details: #>   Units (N)           : 120 #>   Time periods (T)    : 6 #>   Treated cohorts (G) : 3 #>   Covariates (d)      : 2 #>   Features (p)        : 62 #>   Selected size       : 42 #>   Lambda*             : 0.0099 cat("True ATT:        ", true_tes$att_true, "
") #> True ATT:         3.877778 cat("etwfe() ATT:     ", res_etwfe$att_hat, "
") #> etwfe() ATT:      3.88994 cat("betwfe() ATT:    ", res_betwfe$att_hat, "
") #> betwfe() ATT:     3.784618 cat("etwfe sq. error: ", (res_etwfe$att_hat - true_tes$att_true)^2, "
") #> etwfe sq. error:  0.0001479292 cat("betwfe sq. error:", (res_betwfe$att_hat - true_tes$att_true)^2, "
") #> betwfe sq. error: 0.008678791

title: Comparison Estimators: ETWFE and BETWFE
text: examples use panel d = 2 time-invariant covariates. package equally supports -covariate case passing covs = c() estimator (generating data genCoefs(d = 0, ...)). section runs simulated regime covariates, side--side, user can see etwfe() fetwfe() look like simpler setting. etwfe() -covariate setting: fetwfe() -covariate setting: Side--side overall ATT estimates truth: Two qualitative differences note -covariate regime: Smaller design matrix. Without covariates cohort/time/treatment interactions, underlying regression many fewer columns, estimators converge faster etwfe() becomes rank-stable smaller cohort sizes ((d + 1)-units-per-cohort floor etwfe() enforces drops just one unit per cohort). Standard errors typically widen. Covariates explain residual variance absent, idiosyncratic-noise variance sig_eps_sq enters SEs without explanatory cushion. signal--noise ratio treatment-effect coefficients drops, trade-simpler model. package handles covs = c() end--end without special-casing user’s side: data-prep pipeline (prep_for_etwfe_core R/input_prep.R) dispatches d == 0 skips covariate-interaction columns automatically. applies betwfe() twfeCovs().
code:
sim_coefs_d0 <- genCoefs(   G         = 3,   T         = 6,   d         = 0,   density   = 0.5,   eff_size  = 2,   seed      = 20260510 )  sim_data_d0 <- simulateData(   sim_coefs_d0,   N            = 120,   sig_eps_sq   = 1,   sig_eps_c_sq = 1,   seed         = 20260510 )  true_tes_d0 <- getTes(sim_coefs_d0) cat("True overall ATT (no covariates):", true_tes_d0$att_true, "
") #> True overall ATT (no covariates): 2.866667 res_etwfe_d0 <- etwfeWithSimulatedData(sim_data_d0) summary(res_etwfe_d0) #> Summary of Extended Two-Way Fixed Effects #> Pooling weights sample-cohort-size weights (matching ::aggte(type = "dynamic") convention). variance combines regression-coefficient term cohort-probability term, mirroring package’s existing overall-ATT SE machinery. Event-time estimates etwfe():  Event-time estimates betwfe() simulated panel:  eventStudy() returns underlying data; plot() returns ggplot2 object can customize. ggplot2 Suggests:, must installed use plot() methods; estimators work without . parallel cohortStudy() accessor returns per-cohort ATT estimates tidy data frame (information available res$catt_df, surfaced discoverable function help page): eventStudy() cohortStudy() support broom::tidy() via dedicated S3 methods; see main fetwfe() vignette broom workflow.

Combines bridge regularization fusion transformation bias variance reduction. See main fetwfe() vignette real-data application simulation vignette simulation workflow. betwfe() — alternative want regularization fusion transformation. Useful inspecting effect fusion alone — compare betwfe() vs. fetwfe() data difference fusion adds. etwfe() — useful baseline. well-conditioned data produces unbiased point estimates valid standard errors. small -parameterized data can fail rank-deficient cohort errors, fetwfe()’s regularization avoids. real-data application recommended estimator, see main fetwfe() vignette.

title: fetwfe: A Package for Fused Extended Two-Way Fixed Effects
text: understand basic idea difference--differences staggered adoptions , need know fused extended two-way fixed effects (FETWFE) get started using fetwfe package : given appropriately formatted panel data set, fetwfe() give estimate overall average treatment effect treated units, average treatment effect within cohort, standard errors estimates. Feel free skip “Package Usage” section want jump right using package. next “Background” subsection, can read little background information methodology ’d like.

title: fetwfe: A Package for Fused Extended Two-Way Fixed Effects
text: vignette written assumption ’re least vaguely familiar developments difference--differences staggered adoptions since 2018. Just make sure ’re page, brief recap : Historically, staggered adoptions researchers used standard two-way fixed effects estimator interpreted coefficient treatment dummy average treatment effect treated units. late 2010s, econometricians formally checked estimator found fact, coefficient kind reasonable average treatment effect estimator. Since , number new difference--differences estimators asymptotically unbiased staggered adoptions developed. estimator package, fused extended two-way fixed effects (FETWFE), one asymptotically unbiased estimators. course, made estimator think FETWFE brings something table others don’t. ’s brief summary : One issue estimators ’ve worked hard unbiased inefficient (language econometrics), high-variance (language machine learning). estimators add extra parameters order remove bias, estimating extra parameters means less data per parameter estimates noisier. machine learning, creating flexible estimator lots parameters finding high variance (, overfits) familiar issue. common solution regularization. just add ℓ2\ell_2 ℓ1\ell_1 regularization difference--differences regression estimator probably see improvement efficiency, FETWFE something sophisticated . (Plus, approach wouldn’t allow get valid standard errors treatment effect estimates, FETWFE .) Qualitatively, FETWFE uses machine learning learn added parameters actually unnecessary add, takes back order improve efficiency. ’s description ’ll give vignette. can learn details paper arXiv: Fused Extended Two-Way Fixed Effects Difference--Differences Staggered Adoptions want learn little dive full paper, resources descriptions methodology provide little detail vignette: blog post announcing paper. slides made presentation FETWFE. Another blog post focused package hood. headline summary fused extended two-way fixed effects brings table crowded field estimators : fused extended two-way fixed effects unbiased, also uses machine learning maximize efficiency (minimize variance). , unlike many machine learning estimators, fused extended two-way fixed effects gives valid standard errors treatment effect estimates.

title: fetwfe: A Package for Fused Extended Two-Way Fixed Effects
text: package provides single exported function, fetwfe(), implements FETWFE estimator. primary arguments include: pdata: data frame panel (long) format. time_var: character string specifying name time period variable. unit_var: character string specifying unit (e.g. state, firm) variable. treatment: character string specifying treatment indicator variable (must absorbing binary indicator). response: character string specifying response (outcome) variable. covs: character vector covariate names (typically time-invariant pre-treatment values), applicable. Additional tuning parameters: tuning parameter bridge penalty (controlled via argument q), geometry fusion penalty (fusion_structure, either default "cohort" "event_study", fully custom fusion_matrix), options verbosity, standard error calculation, . function returns list containing, example, estimated overall average treatment effect, cohort-specific treatment effects, standard errors (available), various diagnostic quantities. can get full documentation details using ?fetwfe R package loaded. workflow three moves. Working fit comes : Fit — call fetwfe() panel. Extract — pull treatment effects view want: cohortStudy() (per adoption cohort), eventStudy() (per time since treatment), cohortTimeATTs() (fully disaggregated cohort--time cells); overall ATT fit att_hat / att_se. Plot — visualize plot(fit, type = "event_study") plot(fit, type = "catt"). rest vignette expands . detailed discussion package’s standard errors computed, assumptions rely , experimental cluster-robust option (se_type = "cluster") suitable sensitivity check, see companion vignette inference_vignette. Two companion vignettes cover family-wise simultaneous confidence bands (simultaneousCIs()) selection-consistency zero-effect test (simultaneous_cis_vignette), high-dimensional debiased inference via debiasedATT() (high_dimensional_vignette). next sections, ’ll walk examples fetwfe() used.

title: fetwfe: A Package for Fused Extended Two-Way Fixed Effects
text: ’ll start illustrating use fetwfe() using simulated data set. example simulates balanced panel 20 time periods 30 individuals. individual assigned random one five cohort levels; randomly drawn treatment timing, three levels resolve cohorts actually treated within panel window rest never treated. simulation, individual assigned random cohort (determines timing treatment) three time-invariant covariates generated. response variable constructed , treatment, evolution depends treatment effect (varies cohort) linear trend, plus covariates random noise. complete code simulating data, converting required pdata format, running fetwfe() function. borrowed code Asjad Naqvi’s helpful website estimators. Thanks sharing code publicly! simulated data (dat) now columns time, id, treatment indicator (treat), three time-invariant covariates (cov1, cov2, cov3), response variable (y). Next, convert data panel data format required fetwfe(). Now pdata properly formatted, run FETWFE estimator simulated data. run code, function internally performs necessary data preparation, applies fusion penalty via bridge regression (using grpreg package), returns list overall cohort-specific treatment effect estimates, standard errors (available), additional diagnostics. default fusion penalty uses within-/-cohort structure, can instead fuse treatment effects event time (time since treatment) fusion_structure = "event_study", supply fully custom fusion matrix. prefer , see vignette “Choosing fusion structure: cohort vs. event-study penalties” (vignette("fusion_structure_vignette", package = "fetwfe")).
code: # Set seed for reproducibility set.seed(123456L)  # 20 time periods, 30 individuals, and 5 cohort levels tmax = 20; imax = 30; nlvls = 5  dat =   expand.grid(time = 1:tmax, id = 1:imax) |>   within({     cohort      = NA     effect      = NA     first_treat = NA     cov1        = NA     cov2        = NA     cov3        = NA     for (chrt in 1:imax) {       cohort = ifelse(id==chrt, sample.int(nlvls, 1), cohort)     }     for (lvls in 1:nlvls) {       effect      = ifelse(cohort==lvls, sample(2:10, 1), effect)       first_treat = ifelse(cohort==lvls, sample(1:(tmax+6), 1), first_treat)     }     # three time-invariant covariates: one value per individual,     # drawn AFTER the timing loop so first_treat is unchanged     for (chrt in 1:imax) {       cov1 = ifelse(id==chrt, rnorm(1), cov1)       cov2 = ifelse(id==chrt, rnorm(1), cov2)       cov3 = ifelse(id==chrt, rnorm(1), cov3)     }     first_treat = ifelse(first_treat>tmax, Inf, first_treat)     treat       = time >= first_treat     rel_time    = time - first_treat     y           = id + time + ifelse(treat, effect*rel_time, 0) +                   0.5*cov1 - 0.7*cov2 + 1.2*cov3 + rnorm(imax*tmax)     rm(chrt, lvls, cohort, effect)   })  head(dat) ##   time id         y rel_time treat       cov3      cov2       cov1 first_treat ## 1    1  1 1.3403587     -Inf FALSE -0.5019485 0.1582893 -0.8962503         Inf ## 2    2  1 0.2842639     -Inf FALSE -0.5019485 0.1582893 -0.8962503         Inf ## 3    3  1 3.7222320     -Inf FALSE -0.5019485 0.1582893 -0.8962503         Inf ## 4    4  1 3.6309664     -Inf FALSE -0.5019485 0.1582893 -0.8962503         Inf ## 5    5  1 6.6701082     -Inf FALSE -0.5019485 0.1582893 -0.8962503         Inf ## 6    6  1 6.0159986     -Inf FALSE -0.5019485 0.1582893 -0.8962503         Inf library(dplyr)  # Specify column names for the pdata format time_var <- "time"       # Column for the time period unit_var <- "unit"       # Column for the unit identifier treatment <- "treated"   # Column for the treatment dummy indicator response <- "response"   # Column for the response variable  # Convert the dataset pdata <- dat |>   mutate(     # Rename id to unit and convert to character     {{ unit_var }} := as.character(id),     # Ensure treatment dummy is 0/1     {{ treatment }} := as.integer(treat),     # Rename y to response     {{ response }} := y   ) |>   select(     {{ time_var }}, {{ unit_var }}, {{ treatment }}, {{ response }},     cov1, cov2, cov3   )  # Preview the resulting pdata dataframe head(pdata) ##   time unit treated  response       cov1      cov2       cov3 ## 1    1    1       0 1.3403587 -0.8962503 0.1582893 -0.5019485 ## 2    2    1       0 0.2842639 -0.8962503 0.1582893 -0.5019485 ## 3    3    1       0 3.7222320 -0.8962503 0.1582893 -0.5019485 ## 4    4    1       0 3.6309664 -0.8962503 0.1582893 -0.5019485 ## 5    5    1       0 6.6701082 -0.8962503 0.1582893 -0.5019485 ## 6    6    1       0 6.0159986 -0.8962503 0.1582893 -0.5019485 library(fetwfe)  # Run the FETWFE estimator on the simulated data result <- fetwfe(   pdata = pdata,                        # The panel dataset   time_var = "time",                    # The time variable   unit_var = "unit",                    # The unit identifier   treatment = "treated",                # The treatment dummy indicator   response = "response",                # The response variable   covs = c("cov1", "cov2", "cov3")      # The time-invariant covariates )  # Display the average treatment effect estimates summary(result) ## Summary of Fused Extended Two-Way Fixed Effects ## ================================================ ##  ## Overall ATT: 30.4626  (SE = 4.1889, p = 3.535e-13, 95% CI = [22.2525, 38.6726]) ## Selected: TRUE ## title: fetwfe: A Package for Fused Extended Two-Way Fixed Effects
text: Next illustrate FETWFE empirical context. ’ll use divorce data set bacondecomp package, comes study Stevenson Wolfers (2006) unilateral (“-fault”) divorce laws female suicide rates. balanced state-year panel covering 51 states 1964-1996, reforms adopted staggered, scattered fashion across states. empirical application Section 8.2 Faletto (2025). FETWFE estimates adopting unilateral divorce law associated roughly -6% change elasticity-scaled female suicide rate, 95% confidence interval excludes zero. sign order magnitude match Stevenson Wolfers (2006), found reforms reduced female suicide. Unlike fully fused fit, FETWFE’s selection step retains heterogeneous cohort effects : several adoption cohorts pruned exactly zero rest carry nonzero, varying estimates, rows res$catt_df longer identical. Unlike simulated example , fit includes covariates (covs argument). FETWFE regularizes cohort-interacted design bridge penalty, accommodates covariates even panel’s scattered, single-state adoption cohorts – setting unregularized two-way fixed effects fit rank-deficient.

title: fetwfe: A Package for Fused Extended Two-Way Fixed Effects
text: Empirical users difference--differences routinely want ask “treatment effect statistically distinguishable zero?”. confidence intervals catt_df answer implicitly — CI excludes 0 corresponds rejecting H_0: tau = 0 level alpha (family-wise across cohorts default simultaneous bands). package also surfaces per-cohort p_value matches whichever interval displayed: default ci_type = "simultaneous" single-step max-T multiplicity-adjusted p-value (exact dual simultaneous band, CI-excludes-0 test p_value < alpha always agree; version 1.18.0), ci_type = "pointwise" two-sided Wald 2 * pnorm(-|estimate / se|). overall-ATT att_p_value scalar Wald p-value (single effect, multiplicity). fetwfe() betwfe() also selected logical flag, overall ATT level per-cohort. fetwfe(), selected flag carries something stronger usual CI test. restriction selection consistency theorem (Theorem 6.2 paper), true tau_ATT(g, t) = 0, estimator returns exactly 0 probability tending 1. package’s interpretation: selected = FALSE cohort, asymptotic conclusion truth zero — strictly stronger statement standard CI provides. package surfaces asymptotic 100% confidence interval {0} selected-cohorts. selected-cohorts, p_value reported NA — inferential content lives selected. betwfe() uses bridge regression directly coefficients (rather fused restrictions used fetwfe()). bridge oracle property Kock (2013), selected = FALSE betwfe() analogous asymptotic statement truth zero, sparsity assumption different one Theorem 6.2 establishes fetwfe(). etwfe() twfeCovs(), neither selection step, selected column omitted; p_value column standard post-OLS t-test. worth keeping mind asymptotic 100% CI {0} interpretation N -> infinity statement. small samples, may wise read selected p_value together rather relying either alone. particular, even selected = TRUE, standard CI may cover zero — selection step says “truth nonzero” magnitude CI says “zero”.
code: set.seed(2026) sim <- genCoefs(G = 3, T = 6, d = 2, density = 0.5, eff_size = 2) # sim was built without a seed, so draw from the ambient generator (seeded # just above); seed = NA selects the ambient generator silently. dat <- simulateData(sim, N = 120, sig_eps_sq = 1, sig_eps_c_sq = 0.5, seed = NA) res_demo <- fetwfeWithSimulatedData(dat, verbose = FALSE) res_demo$catt_df ##   cohort   estimate         se    ci_low    ci_high      p_value selected ## 1      2 -0.4137526 0.07754859 -0.586916 -0.2405893 1.906689e-07     TRUE ## 2      3 -1.6817852 0.12781952 -1.967202 -1.3963687 0.000000e+00     TRUE ## 3      4  0.0000000 0.00000000  0.000000  0.0000000           NA    FALSE

title: Choosing a fusion structure: cohort vs. event-study penalties
text: FETWFE estimates separate treatment effect every treated cohort g every post-treatment period t — written tau_{g, t} — shrinks effects toward fusion penalty. “Fusion” means penalty acts differences selected pairs treatment-effect coefficients: pushing difference toward zero pools two effects, estimator borrows strength across cells instead estimating tau_{g, t} isolation. differences penalty acts fusion structure, encodes kind similarity expect treatment effects . Since version 1.19.0 fetwfe() exposes choice single argument, fusion_structure, two options: penalties data-driven, hard restrictions. estimator pools toward chosen structure free deviate data demand . choosing fusion structure compromise asymptotic validity estimator (lemma paper’s extensions section shows event-study penalty satisfies conditions, singular-value constants sigma_max <= sqrt(6) sigma_min >= 1 / (T * sqrt(2 * T)), default; every consistency, restriction-selection, inference guarantee paper therefore holds verbatim ). cost guessing “wrong” finite-sample efficiency — noisier estimates — bias invalid confidence intervals.
code: fetwfe(..., fusion_structure = "cohort")       # the default fetwfe(..., fusion_structure = "event_study")  # the alternative added for issue #40

title: Choosing a fusion structure: cohort vs. event-study penalties
text: default penalty fuses adjacent cohorts calendar time adjacent time periods within cohort. pools toward assumption treatment effects vary smoothly across neighboring cohorts smoothly calendar time within cohort. sensible, agnostic default: privilege single summary effects.

title: Choosing a fusion structure: cohort vs. event-study penalties
text: event-study penalty instead fuses, event time e = 0, 1, 2, ... (time since treatment), effect tau_{r, r + e} across cohorts r. pools toward assumption treatment effects depend time since treatment, calendar period cohort — identifying restriction behind standard event-study aggregation used throughout applied difference--differences (e.g., ::aggte(type = "dynamic")). data really driven event time (gradual onset, dose-response accumulates exposure), pools strength across cohorts event time estimates dynamic path efficiently.

what: Two meanings of “event study” — read this first
title: Choosing a fusion structure: cohort vs. event-study penalties
text: word “event study” appears fetwfe two distinct, independent places, conflating common source confusion: fusion_structure = "event_study" estimation-time penalty. changes fit — coefficients get fetwfe(). family = "event_study" eventStudy() simultaneousCIs(fit, family = "event_study") reporting-time aggregation. changes already-fitted set effects summarized (one number per event time), nothing fit. two choices orthogonal: can report event-study aggregation cohort-penalized fit, cohort aggregation event-study-penalized fit. Every combination valid: rest vignette uses eventStudy() (reporting aggregation) display fits made penalties — concrete illustration orthogonality.
code: # event-time SUMMARY of a default (cohort-penalized) fit: eventStudy(fetwfe(..., fusion_structure = "cohort"))  # cohort SUMMARY of an event-study-penalized fit: simultaneousCIs(fetwfe(..., fusion_structure = "event_study"), family = "cohort")

title: Choosing a fusion structure: cohort vs. event-study penalties
text: simulate panel whose treatment effects sparse event-study basis — effects share time since treatment (event time e) fused across cohorts, true effect depends mostly e. generate truth natively genCoefs(fusion_structure = "event_study") (simulation-side companion estimator argument), fit panel fusion structures see recovers truth better. know data-generating effects exactly, can measure recovery error directly. use package’s simulation utilities (genCoefs() / simulateData() / fetwfeWithSimulatedData(); see Simulation vignette full tour). treatment-effect coefficients occupy known block coefficient vector; fetwfe() reports positions treat_inds slot (see ?fetwfe), positions depend panel shape (G cohorts, T periods, d covariates): Now fit simulated panel fusion structure: well fit recover true per-cell effects? Compare estimated treatment-effect coefficients known truth (mean squared error 33 cells): panel event-study penalty cuts per-cell recovery error 67%. advantage exactly expect: truth event-study structure, pooling effects across cohorts event time pools right things, default penalty spends effort smoothing across cohorts calendar time truth actually share. story visible reporting level. aggregate fits one effect per event time eventStudy() (reporting family — applied top penalties) overlay true profile:  event-study-penalized profile tracks dashed truth closely; default profile lags, calendar--cohort smoothing pulls effects toward neighbors truth actually share. (exact magnitude — , single draw, even direction — depends simulated data: across event-study-sparse draws configuration event-study penalty wins roughly four five seeds, per-cell coefficient recovery , aggregated profile, captures reliably. seed shown one representative draw.)
code: G <- 6L T <- 9L d <- 1L N <- 400L  # Generate truth that is sparse in the event-study basis directly, with # fusion_structure = "event_study". seed 10 is a representative draw (see the # note after the plot); density 0.25 keeps the truth genuinely sparse in the # event-study basis. coefs <- genCoefs(   G = G, T = T, d = d, density = 0.25, eff_size = 2, seed = 10,   fusion_structure = "event_study" )  # Locate the treatment-effect block tau_{g, t} (cohort-major order). cohort_times <- getTes(coefs)$cohort_times # each cohort's first treated period n_k <- T - cohort_times + 1 # number of post-treatment effects per cohort num_treats <- sum(n_k) base_cols <- G + (T - 1) + d + d * G + d * (T - 1) # fixed effects + covariate terms # `fetwfe()` reports these positions as an integer vector, so build `treat_inds` # as an integer too (the `base_cols` arithmetic above is double-typed) -- this # keeps the `identical()` cross-check below exact rather than tripping on storage # mode. treat_inds <- as.integer(base_cols + seq_len(num_treats)) # the tau block  # Event time (time since treatment) of each tau, in the same order. event_time <- unlist(lapply(n_k, function(nk) 0:(nk - 1)))  # The true per-cell treatment effects, generated natively (not hand-built). true_te <- coefs$beta[treat_inds]  sim <- simulateData(coefs, N = N, sig_eps_sq = 1, sig_eps_c_sq = 0.5, seed = 10) fit_cohort <- fetwfeWithSimulatedData(sim, lambda_selection = "bic") fit_event <- fetwfeWithSimulatedData(   sim,   lambda_selection = "bic",   fusion_structure = "event_study" )  # The positions we wrote the truth to are exactly the ones fetwfe() reports as # the treatment effects, so we can compare estimates to the truth cell by cell. stopifnot(identical(treat_inds, fit_cohort$treat_inds)) mse <- function(fit) mean((fit$beta_hat[treat_inds] - true_te)^2) errs <- c(cohort = mse(fit_cohort), event_study = mse(fit_event)) round(errs, 3) #>      cohort event_study  #>       0.013       0.004 library(ggplot2) library(dplyr) #>  #> Attaching package: 'dplyr' #> The following objects are masked from 'package:stats': #>  #>     filter, lag #> The following objects are masked from 'package:base': #>  #>     intersect, setdiff, setequal, union  profiles <- dplyr::bind_rows(   as.data.frame(eventStudy(fit_cohort)) |>     dplyr::mutate(structure = "cohort (default)"),   as.data.frame(eventStudy(fit_event)) |>     dplyr::mutate(structure = "event_study") ) # True profile per event time: the mean of the natively generated tau at each # event time (the truth is event-study-sparse, not a clean formula line). truth <- aggregate(   estimate ~ event_time,   data = data.frame(event_time = event_time, estimate = true_te),   FUN = mean )  ggplot(profiles, aes(event_time, estimate, color = structure, fill = structure)) +   geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = ci_low, ymax = ci_high), alpha = 0.15, color = NA) +   geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +   geom_point(size = 1.6) +   geom_line(     data = truth, aes(event_time, estimate),     color = "black", linetype = "dashed", inherit.aes = FALSE   ) +   labs(     title = "Recovering an event-time treatment-effect profile",     subtitle = "dashed black = true event-time profile (mean generated tau by event time)",     x = "event time (periods since treatment)",     y = "treatment effect",     color = "fusion_structure", fill = "fusion_structure"   ) +   theme_minimal()

title: Choosing a fusion structure: cohort vs. event-study penalties
text: Prefer fusion_structure = "event_study" believe treatment effects driven mainly time since treatment — gradual onset, build-decay effect exposure — want pool strength across cohorts event time. matches default null applied event-study literature, estimator’s “shrink toward event-study structure” behavior familiar story report. Prefer fusion_structure = "cohort" (default) treatment effects plausibly differ cohort — cohort-specific responsiveness, calendar-time shocks hit cohorts given year — want smoothness across neighboring cohorts within-cohort time without privileging event-time summary. right agnostic choice strong prior event time thing matters. Either way, penalty data-driven paper’s guarantees hold , decision affects efficiency, validity. unsure, default safe starting point; switch "event_study" dynamic, time-since- treatment path object interest expect shared across cohorts.

what: Supplying your own fusion matrix D_N (advanced)
title: Choosing a fusion structure: cohort vs. event-study penalties
text: two built-structures special cases general mechanism: hood, fetwfe() applies forward-differences matrix D_N treatment-effect block, penalizes differenced coordinates, maps back via solve(D_N). fusion_matrix argument lets supply num_treats x num_treats D_N directly — encoding arbitrary identifying-restriction structure (example, “fuse cohorts share industry”) neither built-expresses. supplied, fusion_matrix overrides fusion_structure treatment-effect block (fixed-effect blocks unchanged).

previous_headings: Supplying your own fusion matrix D_N (advanced)
title: Choosing a fusion structure: cohort vs. event-study penalties
text: D_N indexed position, rows columns must follow cohort-major (g, t) order estimator uses treatment effects. Row/column corresponds base treatment effect : num_treats = T * G - G * (G + 1) / 2 total number base treatment effects. getFirstInds(G, T) returns, cohort g, 1-based row first effect tau_{g, 0}. Cohort g occupies rows first_inds[g]:(first_inds[g + 1] - 1) (last cohort runs num_treats), ordered event time within cohort (tau_{g, 0}, tau_{g, 1}, ...). getTreatInds(G, T, d, num_treats) maps block full design-matrix columns; fit$treat_inds reports positions fitted object. Interpreting D_N forward differences: theta = D_N %*% beta penalized (sparse) parameterization beta = solve(D_N) %*% theta recovers original-scale effects. supply D_N; code uses solve(D_N).

previous_headings: Supplying your own fusion matrix D_N (advanced)
title: Choosing a fusion structure: cohort vs. event-study penalties
text: build first_inds per-cohort effect counts n_k (already computed ; internal helper needed) matrix’s cohort-major layout explicit, supply custom D_N refit. (reuses sim panel side--side demo .) genuinely bespoke D_N — one fuses set effects (example, event-study structure, “fuse cohorts share industry”) — built way: choose differences penalize, assemble corresponding forward-differences matrix cohort-major order, pass fusion_matrix.
code: # first_inds in the same cohort-major order the matrix must follow: cohort 1 # starts at row 1, and each later cohort starts after the previous cohort's # block (of size n_k). first_inds <- cumsum(c(1, head(n_k, -1))) first_inds # the 1-based starting row of each cohort's first effect #> [1]  1  9 16 22 27 31  # A within-cohort backward-differences D_N: penalize each cohort's first effect # directly (toward zero) and every later effect toward its within-cohort # predecessor. (theta_i = beta_i - beta_{i-1} within a cohort; theta = beta at # each cohort's first effect.) This is one transparent, hand-built example; any # invertible num_treats x num_treats matrix in this row order is accepted. D_N <- diag(num_treats) for (k in seq_along(first_inds)) {   start <- first_inds[k]   end <- if (k < length(first_inds)) first_inds[k + 1] - 1 else num_treats   if (end > start) {     for (i in (start + 1):end) {       D_N[i, i - 1] <- -1     }   } }  fit_custom <- fetwfeWithSimulatedData(   sim,   lambda_selection = "bic",   fusion_matrix = D_N )  # A finite, well-formed fit; the custom block was used throughout. fit_custom$att_hat #> [1] 0.6717676

previous_headings: Supplying your own fusion matrix D_N (advanced)
title: Choosing a fusion structure: cohort vs. event-study penalties
text: paper’s fixed-dimension scoping, inferential guarantees (valid standard errors confidence intervals) hold finite, invertible D_N: theory depends D_N invertibility size singular values (Assumption (D) Faletto 2025), fixed invertible matrix automatically singular values fixed positive range. Swapping one valid D_N another changes constant factors, guarantees — need engineer D_N hit particular singular-value targets. Finite invertible enough. fetwfe() enforces exactly , nothing stricter. stops fusion_matrix finite, invertible numeric matrix exact num_treats x num_treats dimension — malformed restriction matrix usage error, modeling choice. numerical courtesy also emits warning() (error) supplied D_N near-singular: still invertible, estimation proceeds, inverse may numerically unreliable, usually means better-conditioned D_N serve better.
code: # A near-singular (but invertible) D_N: estimation proceeds, with a heads-up. near_singular <- diag(num_treats) near_singular[1, 1] <- 1e-12 fit_warned <- tryCatch(   fetwfeWithSimulatedData(sim, lambda_selection = "bic", fusion_matrix = near_singular),   warning = function(w) conditionMessage(w) ) # The warning message (estimation still succeeds if you allow the warning): cat(fit_warned) #> fusion_matrix is numerically near-singular (reciprocal condition number = 1e-12 < 1e-10). It is still invertible and yields a valid point estimator, but solve(fusion_matrix) may be numerically unreliable; consider a better-conditioned D_N.

title: High-dimensional inference: debiasedATT() when p >= NT
text: standard errors simultaneous bands documented companion vignettes assume low-dimensional design (p<NTp < NT), analytic Gram inverse exists fit’s standard errors asymptotically valid ones vignette("inference_vignette"). design high-dimensional — number design columns pp (cohort time fixed effects, covariates, treatment interactions) meets exceeds number observations NTNT — two things change: fused fit regularized (bridge) estimate, pointwise standard errors (simultaneous bands vignette("simultaneous_cis_vignette")) post-selection: valid conditional selected support, uniformly valid. can -cover, bridge’s regularization biases point estimate (shrinks prunes effects) post-selection standard error account selection. debiasedATT() returns uniformly valid confidence interval overall ATT desparsifying fit (nodewise relaxed-inverse construction high-dimensional FETWFE theory Faletto 2025), removes regularization bias. “one estimator, two regimes”: call returned object identical p<NTp < NT case; internal nuisance changes. p≥NTp \geq NT regime arises many covariates, short panels, many cohorts. simulateData() can generate panels.

title: High-dimensional inference: debiasedATT() when p >= NT
text: fused estimate post-selection. uniformly valid inference, pass fit debiasedATT(), implements high-dimensional debiased construction Theorem debiased.highdim.thm (Faletto 2025). coverage robust across penalty values studied — cross-validated selector theory scale fixed constant bracket default 1.0 — default fine; let lambda_c = "cv" select constant per fit alternative. print surfaces high-dimensional diagnostics worth inspecting: resolved nodewise penalty (lambda_c / lambda_node) , per debiasing direction, whether converged met KKT feasibility certificate. direction fails converge stay feasible flags potentially unreliable standard error — raise riesz_max_iter /lambda_c re-inspect.
code: # d = 12 covariates, N = 40 units, T = 5 periods, G = 3 cohorts. coefs_hd <- genCoefs(G = 3, T = 5, d = 12, density = 0.2, eff_size = 2, seed = 2) sim_hd <- simulateData(   coefs_hd, N = 40, sig_eps_sq = 1, sig_eps_c_sq = 0.5, seed = 2 ) c(p = sim_hd$p, NT = sim_hd$N * sim_hd$T) # p >= NT: high-dimensional #>   p  NT  #> 220 200 fit_hd <- fetwfeWithSimulatedData(sim_hd, q = 0.5) # The fused (post-selection, pointwise) overall ATT: round(c(att = fit_hd$att_hat, se = fit_hd$att_se), 3) #>    att     se  #> -2.416  0.186 db <- debiasedATT(fit_hd, lambda_c = "cv") db #> Debiased overall ATT (high-dimensional regime) #>   Estimate:   -2.3684 #>   Std. Error: 0.2507 #>   95% CI: [-2.8597, -1.8771] #> High-dimensional (p >= NT) desparsified interval #>   [overall-ATT coverage validated near-nominally in simulation #>   (Theorem debiased.highdim.thm, Faletto 2025); diagnostics below] #>   nodewise penalty: lambda_c = 0.5 (CV-selected); lambda_node in [0.1836, 0.1836] #>   nodewise directions: 1/1 converged, 1/1 KKT-feasible (worst feasibility/lambda_node = 1)

what: Why debiased, and how well it covers
title: High-dimensional inference: debiasedATT() when p >= NT
text: single draw fused debiased point estimates can close, value debiasedATT() visible one fit — repeated-sampling guarantee. coverage study p>NTp > NT anchor (reported Faletto 2025) found: fused interval -covers post-selection non-uniformity; debiased interval near-nominal — direct simulation confirmation Theorem debiased.highdim.thm, robust across penalty values studied (CV selector theory scale fixed constant , bracketing default 1.0). per-direction converged / feasibility diagnostics remain worth glance confirm nodewise directions well-behaved fit.

what: An asymptotic alternative: the wild bootstrap
title: High-dimensional inference: debiasedATT() when p >= NT
text: method = "bootstrap" offers alternative, asymptotically-valid reference distribution overall-ATT interval: studentized score / influence-function wild cluster bootstrap re-signs per-unit influence summands (refit per replicate), leaving point estimate reported se unchanged changing critical value. floored Gaussian quantile, interval never narrower analytic Wald interval. -clusters remedy. analytic sandwich SE downward-biased clusters, bootstrap fix : unrestricted, -refit score bootstrap reproduce tail inflation restricted wild-cluster bootstrap-t uses -cluster coverage, heterogeneous cluster influence critical value (floor) actually falls Gaussian — floor reduces analytic interval exactly small-N regime. effect widen interval cluster influence near-homogeneous. genuine -clusters inference restricted bootstrap-t CR2-type analytic correction required (future work). multiplier ("webb", default; "rademacher"; "mammen") affects near-homogeneous case bootstrap widens floor — small-N / heterogeneous regime interval analytic one regardless weight. bootstrap defined single-sample fits (real observational panels single-sample), indep_counts two-sample fits.
code: # `fit` must be a single-sample fetwfe() fit -- not an `indep_counts` fit, which # the worked example above (via fetwfeWithSimulatedData) happens to be. debiasedATT(fit, method = "bootstrap", multiplier = "webb", seed = 1)

title: High-dimensional inference: debiasedATT() when p >= NT
text: simultaneousCIs() also switches desparsified construction high-dimensional regime (via method = "bootstrap"; analytic Gram-inverse band exist p≥NTp \geq NT). Pass method = "bootstrap" valid band: analytic default — eventStudy(ci_type = "simultaneous") — instead return post-selection band selected support, -covers. gls = FALSE fit bootstrap band available. regimes now simulation-validated: Theorem debiased.highdim.thm covers scalar overall-ATT (debiasedATT(), ≈0.94), Theorem debiased.highdim.joint.thm covers family-wise band (simultaneousCIs(method = "bootstrap")) — family-wise coverage ≈0.92 event-study family (K=7K = 7), tighter Bonferroni — p>NTp > NT anchor Faletto (2025). Like scalar, band validated single high-dimensional anchor rests sparsity / restricted-eigenvalue assumptions (assumed, proved); cohort -post-treatment families covered theorem separately simulated.
code: sc_hd <- simultaneousCIs(   fit_hd,   family = "cohort",   method = "bootstrap",   B = 500,   seed = 1 ) sc_hd$regime #> [1] "high-dimensional"

title: High-dimensional inference: debiasedATT() when p >= NT
text: default density placement spreads signal across coordinates, pp greatly exceeds number treatment-effect parameters rarely lands signal treatment-effect block. controlled, sparse, non-degenerate high-dimensional data-generating process, genCoefs() offers targeted-sparsity mode (n_signal_cohorts treat_base_levels) places heterogeneous treatment-effect signal directly per-cohort base levels — DGP #88 coverage study uses. See ?genCoefs.

title: Standard errors in fetwfe: the variance decomposition and se_type

code: set.seed(2026) sim_coefs <- genCoefs(G = 3, T = 6, d = 2, density = 0.5, eff_size = 2) # sim_coefs was built without a seed, so draw from the ambient generator # (seeded just above); seed = NA selects the ambient generator silently. sim_data <- simulateData(   sim_coefs,   N = 120,   sig_eps_sq = 1,   sig_eps_c_sq = 0.5,   seed = NA )  res_default <- fetwfe(   pdata = sim_data$pdata,   time_var = sim_data$time_var,   unit_var = sim_data$unit_var,   treatment = sim_data$treatment,   covs = sim_data$covs,   response = sim_data$response,   sig_eps_sq = sim_data$sig_eps_sq,   sig_eps_c_sq = sim_data$sig_eps_c_sq )

what: 3. Experimental: cluster-robust standard errors via se_type = "cluster"
title: Standard errors in fetwfe: the variance decomposition and se_type
text: package’s default standard errors rely model-based covariance structure (paper Assumption F1: ..d. units, idiosyncratic noise plus unit random effect). applied work unusual suspect one violations F1: Within-unit serial correlation beyond random effect. unit random effect cic_i F1 absorbs single, time-constant deviation per unit. model serial correlation idiosyncratic shocks. Bertrand, Duflo, Mullainathan (2004) classic warning ignoring within-unit serial correlation can drastically understate standard errors. Heteroskedasticity across units. F1 imposes single σε2\sigma_\varepsilon^2 across units. Higher-level clustering. F1 treats units ..d.; observations within state-year industry-year share unobserved shocks, unit-level F1 variance understate true sampling variability. Starting version 1.6.0, four estimators accept experimental se_type argument value "cluster": Setting se_type = "cluster" swaps model-based regression-coefficient variance V1V_1 unit-clustered Liang-Zeger CR1 sandwich computed bridge-selected support. V2V_2 piece (Ψ-)-tight combination V1+V2V_1 + V_2 unchanged — V1V_1 formula switches.
code: fetwfe(..., se_type = "cluster")

what: 4. Choosing the bridge penalty parameter lambda
title: Standard errors in fetwfe: the variance decomposition and se_type
text: v1.13.0, fetwfe() betwfe() select bridge penalty lambda via 10-fold cross-validation default (using grpreg::cv.grpreg). prior default BIC grpreg lambda grid. change addresses finite-sample bias issue documented simulation studies (see issue #164 package repository): prior BIC default, overall ATT estimator biased toward zero moderate sample sizes, producing 95% confidence intervals whose empirical coverage low 0.00 regimes. Cross-validation restores near-nominal coverage every regime tested additionally lowest MSE high-dim regime FETWFE originally designed . lambda_star lambda_star_model_size result slots populated identically selection paths; underlying selection criterion differs. CV path stores provenance three new top-level slots: lambda_selection, cv_folds, cv_seed. seed defaults .integer(N * T) consecutive calls dataset reproducible without user specify seed. recover prior BIC behavior — reproducing results analyses run v1.12.0 earlier, example — pass lambda_selection = "bic": control CV fold assignment explicitly, pass cv_folds /cv_seed:
code: fit_bic <- fetwfe(..., lambda_selection = "bic") fit_cv <- fetwfe(..., cv_folds = 20L, cv_seed = 42L)

title: Coming from did or etwfe? Bringing your data into fetwfe
text: ::att_gt() identifies panel outcome (yname), time (tname), unit (idname), first-treated cohort (gname, 0 never-treated units). canonical example mpdta — county-level teen employment minimum wage: One line converts , fit fetwfe() usual:
code: library(did) data(mpdta) head(mpdta[, c("countyreal", "year", "first.treat", "lemp")]) #>     countyreal year first.treat     lemp #> 866       8001 2003        2007 8.461469 #> 841       8001 2004        2007 8.336870 #> 842       8001 2005        2007 8.340217 #> 819       8001 2006        2007 8.378161 #> 827       8001 2007        2007 8.487352 #> 937       8019 2003        2007 4.997212 library(fetwfe)  fdf <- attgtToFetwfeDf(     mpdta,     yname = "lemp",     tname = "year",     idname = "countyreal",     gname = "first.treat" )  res <- fetwfe(     pdata = as.data.frame(fdf),     time_var = "time_var",     unit_var = "unit_var",     treatment = "treatment",     response = "response" )  round(c(ATT = res$att_hat, SE = res$att_se), 4) #>     ATT      SE  #> -0.0387  0.0123

title: Coming from did or etwfe? Bringing your data into fetwfe
text: etwfe::etwfe() uses long-panel shape, identified yvar / tvar / idvar / gvar — gvar first-treated period (0 never-treated). etwfeToFetwfeDf() parallel converter. Since mpdta already shape (first.treat cohort variable), converts identically:
code: edf <- etwfeToFetwfeDf(     mpdta,     yvar = "lemp",     tvar = "year",     idvar = "countyreal",     gvar = "first.treat" )  # Byte-identical to the fetwfe-ready panel from the did converter above: identical(edf, fdf) #> [1] TRUE

title: Coming from did or etwfe? Bringing your data into fetwfe
text: etwfe return full set cohort--time (group-time) treatment effects. FETWFE starts saturated extended-two-way-fixed-effects model, : fuses effects statistically similar across neighboring cohorts time periods — data-driven bias–variance trade-spends fewer effective parameters data support pooling, retains heterogeneity don’t; returns overall ATT asymptotically valid standard errors confidence intervals selection, along per-cohort event-study breakdowns (cohortStudy(), eventStudy()) family-wise simultaneous confidence bands (simultaneousCIs()). converters let keep existing data pipeline add fused estimate alongside / etwfe results. full workflow see introductory vignette (vignette("fetwfe")), choice fusion geometry see “Choosing fusion structure: cohort vs. event-study penalties” (vignette("fusion_structure_vignette")).

title: Simulation Vignette for FETWFE: From Coefficients to True Treatment Effects
text: vignette demonstrates conduct simulation studies fetwfe package. particular, : Generate vector coefficients genCoefs(). coefficients produce unit‐‐time specific responses respect difference‐‐differences assumptions (e.g., conditional parallel trends) sparsity assumptions behind FETWFE. Simulate panel data set simulateData(). Estimate treatment effects via FETWFE estimator fetwfeWithSimulatedData(). Extract true treatment effects using getTes(). workflow follows simulation‐study design outlined paper, may wish skim setup section additional context.

title: Simulation Vignette for FETWFE: From Coefficients to True Treatment Effects
text: complete simulation pipeline, step step.

title: Simulation Vignette for FETWFE: From Coefficients to True Treatment Effects
text: genCoefs() function returns object class "FETWFE_coefs", containing coefficient vector simulation parameters. fusion_structure argument (mirroring fetwfe()) additionally controls basis true treatment effects sparse — "cohort" (default) "event_study"; see vignette vignette("fusion_structure_vignette", package = "fetwfe"). example set: G (number treated cohorts) = 3 T (number time periods) = 6 d (number covariates) = 2 density (sparsity level) = 0.1 eff_size (effect‐size multiplier) = 2 (, details meaning parameters, see simulation study section paper.)
code: # Generate the coefficient object for simulation sim_coefs <- genCoefs(   G         = 3,    T         = 4,    d         = 2,    density   = 0.1,    eff_size  = 2,    seed      = 101 )

what: Step 3: Run the FETWFE Estimator on Simulated Data
title: Simulation Vignette for FETWFE: From Coefficients to True Treatment Effects
text: run estimator simulated data using fetwfeWithSimulatedData(). (get results manually unpacking sim_data passing arguments appropriately fewtfe(). fetwfeWithSimulatedData() just wrapper function takes care us.) can now extract results result way can standard fetwfe() function.
code: result <- fetwfeWithSimulatedData(sim_data) summary(result) #> Summary of Fused Extended Two-Way Fixed Effects #> ================================================ #>  #> Overall ATT: 0.0843  (SE = 0.1372, p = 0.5389, 95% CI = [-0.1845, 0.3531]) #> Selected: TRUE #>  #> CATT (preview) [simultaneous 95% CI]: #>  cohort   estimate        se     ci_low    ci_high      p_value selected #>       2 -1.3369236 0.1972001 -1.7779399 -0.8959073 2.411404e-11     TRUE #>       3  0.9165759 0.1264333  0.6338217  1.1993300 8.366641e-13     TRUE #>       4  0.0000000 0.0000000  0.0000000  0.0000000           NA    FALSE #>  #> Event Study (preview) [simultaneous 95% CI]: #>  event_time n_cohorts   estimate        se     ci_low   ci_high      p_value #>           0         3  0.0000000 0.0000000  0.0000000  0.000000           NA #>           1         2  0.5095182 0.3915374 -0.3636595  1.382696 3.417505e-01 #>           2         1 -2.0053854 0.2958002 -2.6650570 -1.345714 2.411404e-11 #>  #> Model Details: #>   Units (N)           : 60 #>   Time periods (T)    : 4 #>   Treated cohorts (G) : 3 #>   Covariates (d)      : 2 #>   Features (p)        : 38 #>   Selected size       : 4 #>   Lambda*             : 0.0984

title: Simulation Vignette for FETWFE: From Coefficients to True Treatment Effects
text: evaluate estimated ATT, can compute true treatment effects using original coefficient object. getTes() function extracts overall average treatment effect cohort-specific effects. can use calculate metrics evaluate estimated treatment effect, like squared error:
code: # Extract the true treatment effects true_tes <- getTes(sim_coefs)  # Print the true overall treatment effect cat("True Overall ATT:", true_tes$att_true, "\n") #> True Overall ATT: -0.1111111  # Print the cohort-specific treatment effects print(true_tes$actual_cohort_tes) #> [1] -1.333333  1.000000  0.000000 squared_error <- (result$att_hat - true_tes$att_true)^2  cat("Squared error of ATT estimate:", squared_error, "\n") #> Squared error of ATT estimate: 0.03817985

what: Combining the Workflow in One Pipeline
title: Simulation Vignette for FETWFE: From Coefficients to True Treatment Effects
text: can also chain simulation functions together pipe operator. following code generates coefficients, simulates data, runs estimator one pipeline:
code:
coefs <- genCoefs(G = 3, T = 4, d = 2, density = 0.1, eff_size = 2, seed = 2025)  result_piped <- coefs |>   simulateData(N = 60, sig_eps_sq = 1, sig_eps_c_sq = 1, seed = 2025) |>   fetwfeWithSimulatedData()  cat("Estimated Overall ATT from piped workflow:", result_piped$att_hat, "
") #> Estimated Overall ATT from piped workflow: -0.7183584  true_tes_piped <- coefs |> getTes()  # Print the true overall treatment effect cat("True Overall ATT:", true_tes_piped$att_true, "
") #> True Overall ATT: -0.6666667  # Print the squared estimation error squared_error_piped = (result_piped$att_hat - true_tes_piped$att_true)^2  cat("Squared estimation error:", squared_error_piped, "
") #> Squared estimation error: 0.00267203

title: Simulation Vignette for FETWFE: From Coefficients to True Treatment Effects
text: simulator workflow generates panels cohort assignment independent covariates. unit assigned one G+1G + 1 cohorts uniform probability 1/(G+1)1/(G + 1), regardless covariate values. simplest data-generating process (DGP) cohort assignment, many realistic settings, cohort membership correlated observable characteristics — adoption decisions depend firm size, industry, region, . Version 1.14.0 fetwfe introduces two covariate-dependent DGPs genCoefs() via new assignment_type assignment_strength arguments: assignment_type = "marginal" (default): original uniform marginal DGP. Preserves pre-1.14.0 behavior exactly. assignment_type = "multinomial": multinomial-logit propensity-score model. unit’s cohort probability πg(x)=exp⁡(γg⊤x)∑g′=0Gexp⁡(γg′⊤x),g=0,1,…,G,\pi_g(x) = \frac{\exp(\gamma_g^\top x)}{\sum_{g' = 0}^{G} \exp(\gamma_{g'}^\top x)}, \quad g = 0, 1, \ldots, G, γ0≡0\gamma_0 \equiv 0 never-treated reference cohort γg\gamma_g drawn Gaussian (scaled assignment_strength) treated cohort gg. assignment_type = "ordered": ordered-logit (proportional-odds) model exploits natural ordering adoption times. ordinal scale runs treated cohorts temporal-adoption order never-treated top: slot 1 cohort 1 (earliest-adopting cohort), slot 2 cohort 2, …, slot GG cohort GG (latest-adopting cohort), slot G+1G + 1 never-treated. Cumulative probabilities follow standard McCullagh proportional-odds parameterization subtraction convention, P(W′≤g∣x)=plogis(αg−γ⊤x),g=1,…,G,P(W' \le g \mid x) = \mathrm{plogis}(\alpha_g - \gamma^\top x), \quad g = 1, \ldots, G, single shared γ\gamma vector cutpoints αg\alpha_g chosen marginal cohort probabilities approximately uniform 1/(G+1)1/(G + 1). γ⊤x\gamma^\top x increases monotonically, modal cohort traverses ordinal scale order: cohort 1 →\cohort 2 →…→\\ldots \cohort GG→\never-treated. Low γ⊤x\gamma^\top x concentrates mass bottom scale (earliest-adopting cohorts); high γ⊤x\gamma^\top x concentrates mass top (never-treated, later cohorts intermediate values). γ\gamma propensity later treatment-timing scale. DGPs canonical reference models named FETWFE paper (Faletto 2025, line 1016).

title: Simulation Vignette for FETWFE: From Coefficients to True Treatment Effects
text: generate panel adoption probabilities depend covariates via multinomial-logit model. assignment_strength parameter scales logit coefficients: assignment_strength = 0, DGP collapses marginal case (uniform 1/(G+1)1/(G + 1) probabilities, regardless XX); larger values produce stronger covariate-cohort coupling. returned FETWFE_coefs object now carries three new slots: assignment_coefs$coefs d×Gd \times G matrix γg\gamma_g vectors; column gg holds propensity coefficients treated cohort gg. can simulate panel verify empirical cohort proportions reflect covariate-cohort coupling rather uniform marginal assignment: can fit FETWFE panel exactly :
code: sim_coefs_mn <- genCoefs(   G                   = 3,   T                   = 4,   d                   = 2,   density             = 0.1,   eff_size            = 2,   assignment_type     = "multinomial",   assignment_strength = 1.0,   seed                = 101 ) sim_coefs_mn$assignment_type #> [1] "multinomial" sim_coefs_mn$assignment_strength #> [1] 1 str(sim_coefs_mn$assignment_coefs) #> List of 6 #>  $ type                : chr "multinomial" #>  $ strength            : num 1 #>  $ coefs               : num [1:2, 1:3] 0.181 0.785 -1.353 1.983 1.238 ... #>  $ interactions        : NULL #>  $ delta               : NULL #>  $ interaction_strength: NULL sim_mn <- simulateData(   sim_coefs_mn,   N            = 200,   sig_eps_sq   = 1,   sig_eps_c_sq = 1,   seed         = 101 ) cat("Empirical cohort proportions:\n") #> Empirical cohort proportions: print(round(sim_mn$assignments / sum(sim_mn$assignments), 3)) #> [1] 0.285 0.180 0.285 0.250 fit_mn <- fetwfeWithSimulatedData(sim_mn, verbose = FALSE) cat("Estimated overall ATT:", fit_mn$att_hat, "\n") #> Estimated overall ATT: 0.1820036

title: Simulation Vignette for FETWFE: From Coefficients to True Treatment Effects
text: proportional-odds variant uses single shared γ\gamma vector GG cutpoints. appropriate cohorts natural ordering — earlier adopters “like” later adopters never-treated units, instance. ordinal scale runs temporal order never-treated top: slot 1 = cohort 1 (first treated), …, slot GG = cohort GG (last treated), slot G+1G + 1 = never-treated. γ⊤x\gamma^\top x increases, modal cohort moves scale cohort 1 toward never-treated. assignment_coefs$coefs length-dd vector (shared γ\gamma) cutpoints length-GG vector cutpoints αg\alpha_g. cutpoints chosen root-finding marginal-uniform condition: g∈1,…,Gg \1, \ldots, G, package solves αg\alpha_g EX[plogis(αg−γ⊤X)]=g/(G+1)E_X[\mathrm{plogis}(\alpha_g - \gamma^\top X)] = g / (G + 1). guarantees marginal cohort proportions approximately uniform 1/(G+1)1/(G + 1) choice assignment_strength — strength controls conditional coupling XX WW, marginal cohort proportions.
code: sim_coefs_ord <- genCoefs(   G                   = 3,   T                   = 4,   d                   = 2,   density             = 0.1,   eff_size            = 2,   assignment_type     = "ordered",   assignment_strength = 1.0,   seed                = 101 ) str(sim_coefs_ord$assignment_coefs) #> List of 7 #>  $ type                : chr "ordered" #>  $ strength            : num 1 #>  $ coefs               : num [1:2] 0.181 0.785 #>  $ cutpoints           : num [1:3] -1.2532 -0.00765 1.23988 #>  $ interactions        : NULL #>  $ delta               : NULL #>  $ interaction_strength: NULL sim_ord <- simulateData(   sim_coefs_ord,   N            = 200,   sig_eps_sq   = 1,   sig_eps_c_sq = 1,   seed         = 101 ) cat("Empirical cohort proportions (ordered):\n") #> Empirical cohort proportions (ordered): print(round(sim_ord$assignments / sum(sim_ord$assignments), 3)) #> [1] 0.315 0.220 0.250 0.215

title: Simulation Vignette for FETWFE: From Coefficients to True Treatment Effects
text: assignment_strength parameter controls strongly covariates predict cohort membership. side--side comparison cohort distribution three strength levels: assignment_strength = 0 cohort proportions approximately uniform 1/(G+1)=0.251/(G + 1) = 0.25. higher strength, proportions deviate uniform covariate distribution drives assignment.
code: for (s in c(0.0, 1.0, 5.0)) {   coefs_s <- genCoefs(     G                   = 3,     T                   = 4,     d                   = 2,     density             = 0.1,     eff_size            = 2,     assignment_type     = "multinomial",     assignment_strength = s,     seed                = 101   )   sim_s <- simulateData(     coefs_s,     N            = 200,     sig_eps_sq   = 1,     sig_eps_c_sq = 1,     seed         = 101   )   cat(sprintf(     "strength = %.1f -> proportions: %s\n",     s,     paste(       round(sim_s$assignments / sum(sim_s$assignments), 3),       collapse = ", "     )   )) } #> strength = 0.0 -> proportions: 0.32, 0.235, 0.215, 0.23 #> strength = 1.0 -> proportions: 0.285, 0.18, 0.285, 0.25 #> strength = 5.0 -> proportions: 0.3, 0.04, 0.36, 0.3

title: Simulation Vignette for FETWFE: From Coefficients to True Treatment Effects
text: Sometimes propensity model needs nonlinear covariates — example, stress-test estimators assume linear propensity. assignment_interactions argument injects multiplicative interaction terms propensity model ; outcome model continues use original covariates. argument accepts list integer pairs naming columns XX whose product enters propensity: Self-interactions allowed produce quadratic terms xj2x_j^2: interaction columns constructed internally inside propensity model time evaluated; simulated pdata keeps just original dd covariate columns (outcome design matrix unchanged). Pairs canonicalized c(min, max) duplicates silently deduplicated.
code: sim_coefs_int <- genCoefs(   G                               = 3,   T                               = 4,   d                               = 2,   density                         = 0.1,   eff_size                        = 2,   assignment_type                 = "multinomial",   assignment_strength             = 1.0,   assignment_interactions         = list(c(1, 2)),   assignment_interaction_strength = 1.0,   seed                            = 101 ) str(sim_coefs_int$assignment_coefs$interactions) #> List of 1 #>  $ : int [1:2] 1 2 str(sim_coefs_int$assignment_coefs$delta) #>  num [1, 1:3] 0.896 0.254 0.55 sim_coefs_quad <- genCoefs(   G                       = 3,   T                       = 4,   d                       = 2,   density                 = 0.1,   eff_size                = 2,   assignment_type         = "multinomial",   assignment_interactions = list(c(1, 1)),   seed                    = 101 )

title: Simulation Vignette for FETWFE: From Coefficients to True Treatment Effects
text: default marginal DGP, overall true ATT equal-weighted average cohort-specific effects: τATT=1G∑g=1GτATT(g).\tau_\mathrm{ATT} = \frac{1}{G} \sum_{g = 1}^{G} \tau_\mathrm{ATT}(g). covariate-dependent DGPs aggregation must use cohort-share weights instead, matching Faletto (2025) Eq. att.estimator.weighted (line 837). getTes() automatically computes propensity-weighted aggregation input FETWFE_coefs object built non-marginal assignment_type: cohort_weights slot new 1.14.0. marginal DGP exactly uniform 1/G1/G; multinomial ordered DGPs reflects E[πg(X)]/∑g′ treatedE[πg′(X)]E[\pi_g(X)] / \sum_{g' \text{ treated}} E[\pi_{g'}(X)], estimated Monte Carlo integration covariate distribution. per-cohort CATT vector actual_cohort_tes unchanged across DGPs — intrinsic coefficient vector β\beta depend cohorts assigned.
code:
te_mn <- getTes(sim_coefs_mn) cat("Multinomial-DGP cohort weights:
") #> Multinomial-DGP cohort weights: print(round(te_mn$cohort_weights, 3)) #> [1] 0.219 0.426 0.355 cat("Propensity-weighted overall ATT:", round(te_mn$att_true, 4), "
") #> Propensity-weighted overall ATT: 0.1343 cat("(Uniform-weighted ATT for comparison:",     round(mean(te_mn$actual_cohort_tes), 4), ")
") #> (Uniform-weighted ATT for comparison: -0.1111 )

title: Simulation Vignette for FETWFE: From Coefficients to True Treatment Effects
text: density argument spreads true signal uniformly across coefficient coordinates. fine low-dimensional designs, design high-dimensional (p≥NTp \geq NT) — many covariates /short panels — treatment-effect coordinates tiny fraction pp entries, density-based draw almost never lands signal : overall ATT comes (near) zero data-generating process degenerate studying treatment-effect inference. Version 1.48.0 added targeted-sparsity mode genCoefs() (mutually-exclusive arguments n_signal_cohorts treat_base_levels) builds coefficient vector deterministically, placing treatment signal directly cohorts’ fused base levels leaving every coordinate zero. builds sparse, non-degenerate, heterogeneous truth positive cohort-weight variance V2>0V_2 > 0 (, n_signal_cohorts form, guaranteed non-zero overall ATT — free-form treat_base_levels mixed-sign levels cancel zero ATT) — data-generating process behind high-dimensional debiased-ATT coverage study (see vignette("high_dimensional_vignette", package = "fetwfe") debiasedATT()). n_signal_cohorts = k quick form: places heterogeneous signal k cohorts (cohort 1 stays baseline; cohorts 2..(k + 1) receive fused base levels eff_size * (1, 2, ..., k)), giving exactly k non-zero coordinates. full control, treat_base_levels takes explicit length-G vector per-cohort fused base levels — per-cohort ATTs: fusion_structure = "cohort" vector (b_1, ..., b_G) maps cumulative cohort effects cumsum(b), c(0, m, 0) yields cohort ATTs (0, m, m). See ?genCoefs.
code: # A high-dimensional design (d = 12 covariates) with a controlled, sparse, # heterogeneous truth -- signal on 2 cohorts' fused base levels: hd_coefs <- genCoefs(   G = 3, T = 5, d = 12,   density = 0.2, # ignored in targeted-sparsity mode, but still required   eff_size = 2,   n_signal_cohorts = 2,   seed = 2 ) te <- getTes(hd_coefs) # A guaranteed non-zero, heterogeneous truth (which a density-based draw at this # dimension almost never produces): c(overall_ATT = te$att_true) #> overall_ATT  #>    2.666667 te$actual_cohort_tes # per-cohort: baseline 0, then two distinct effects #> [1] 0 2 6
